title: How Coffee Changed the World
summary: A narrator traces the remarkable history of coffee, from its origins in Ethiopia to its role in shaping global trade, intellectual culture, and modern daily life.
body: Few beverages have had as much influence on human history as coffee. What began as a discovery in the highlands of Ethiopia over a thousand years ago has grown into one of the world's most widely traded commodities, connecting farmers, traders, and drinkers across every continent. According to legend, a goat herder named Kaldi noticed that his animals became unusually energetic after eating berries from a particular tree. He brought the berries to a local monastery, where monks made a drink from them and discovered it kept them awake during long evening prayers. From Ethiopia, coffee spread to the Arabian Peninsula, where it was cultivated and traded extensively. By the fifteenth century, coffee was being grown in the Yemeni district of Arabia and was soon reaching Persia, Egypt, and Turkey. Coffeehouses, known as qahveh khaneh, became important social and intellectual spaces where people gathered to discuss news, politics, and ideas. They were sometimes called 'schools of the wise.' When coffee arrived in Europe in the seventeenth century, it quickly replaced ale and wine as the preferred morning drink. Before coffee, many Europeans began their day with alcohol, which did not exactly encourage clear thinking. Coffee had the opposite effect. As coffeehouses spread across London, Paris, and Vienna, they became centres of business and political debate. Lloyd's of London, the famous insurance market, actually began as a coffeehouse where merchants met to arrange deals. The transatlantic coffee trade that followed changed the economies of entire regions. European powers established coffee plantations in their colonies in the Americas, Southeast Asia, and Africa. The demand for labour on these plantations is deeply connected to the history of the slave trade, making coffee's story both remarkable and troubling. Today, coffee is the second most traded commodity in the world after oil. More than two billion cups are drunk every day. The rise of speciality coffee in recent decades has transformed how people think about where their coffee comes from and how it is produced. Consumers are increasingly asking questions about fair trade, environmental impact, and the welfare of the farmers who grow their beans. A humble berry from an Ethiopian hillside has, over centuries, shaped economies, sparked revolutions of thought, and become an inseparable part of daily life for billions of people.

annotations.meaningVi: hàng hóa được giao dịch rộng rãi
annotations.exampleEn: Gold and oil are among the most widely traded commodities in the world.

annotations.meaningVi: được trồng trọt và giao dịch
annotations.exampleEn: Silk was cultivated and traded along the ancient Silk Road.

annotations.meaningVi: không gian trí tuệ, nơi trao đổi tư tưởng
annotations.exampleEn: Universities are meant to be intellectual spaces where ideas can be freely debated.

annotations.meaningVi: đồ uống buổi sáng được ưa chuộng
annotations.exampleEn: Tea remains the preferred morning drink for many people in Britain.

annotations.meaningVi: suy nghĩ rõ ràng, tư duy minh mẫn
annotations.exampleEn: A good night's sleep is essential for clear thinking the next day.

annotations.phrase: centres of business and political debate
annotations.meaningVi: trung tâm kinh doanh và tranh luận chính trị
annotations.exampleEn: Cafés in Vienna became centres of business and political debate in the nineteenth century.

annotations.meaningVi: sắp xếp các giao dịch, thỏa thuận kinh doanh
annotations.exampleEn: Merchants used to meet in coffeehouses to arrange deals.

annotations.meaningVi: thương mại cà phê xuyên Đại Tây Dương
annotations.exampleEn: The transatlantic coffee trade transformed the economies of several Caribbean islands.

annotations.meaningVi: thiết lập đồn điền, lập ra các trang trại trồng trọt
annotations.exampleEn: European powers established plantations in their colonies to supply demand back home.

annotations.meaningVi: có liên hệ sâu sắc với, gắn liền với
annotations.exampleEn: The history of sugar production is deeply connected to the history of slavery.

annotations.meaningVi: cà phê đặc sản, cà phê cao cấp
annotations.exampleEn: Speciality coffee shops focus on the origin and quality of their beans.

annotations.meaningVi: thương mại công bằng, mua bán đảm bảo quyền lợi cho người sản xuất
annotations.exampleEn: She prefers to buy fair trade products to support farmers in developing countries.
Companies are under pressure to reduce the environmental impact of their operations.

annotations.meaningVi: châm ngòi cho các cuộc cách mạng tư tưởng
annotations.exampleEn: The printing press sparked revolutions of thought across Europe.
Music is an inseparable part of many cultural celebrations.

annotations.meaningVi: quả mọng khiêm tốn, hạt nhỏ bé bình thường
annotations.exampleEn: It is remarkable how a humble berry led to one of the world's most valuable industries.

grammarNotes.title: Passive Voice — Describing historical processes
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong bài viết về lịch sử, thể bị động rất phổ biến khi không biết hoặc không cần nhấn mạnh đến người thực hiện hành động. Ví dụ: "coffee was being grown", "coffee was cultivated and traded extensively", "European powers established coffee plantations". Cấu trúc bị động: be + past participle. Thể bị động liên tục quá khứ (was being grown) nhấn mạnh hành động kéo dài trong một giai đoạn lịch sử.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Historical writing frequently uses the passive voice to describe processes and changes without naming the agents. 'Coffee was being grown' (past continuous passive) emphasises the ongoing nature of a historical activity. 'Coffee was cultivated and traded' (simple past passive) describes completed historical events. These structures are common in history, geography, and academic writing.

grammarNotes.title: Relative Clauses — Adding detail and description
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Mệnh đề quan hệ được dùng để thêm thông tin chi tiết vào một danh từ mà không cần bắt đầu câu mới. Ví dụ: "a goat herder named Kaldi noticed that his animals became unusually energetic after eating berries from a particular tree" và "Coffeehouses, known as qahveh khaneh, became important social and intellectual spaces where people gathered". Mệnh đề quan hệ xác định (defining relative clause) dùng that/which/where; mệnh đề quan hệ không xác định (non-defining) dùng dấu phẩy và which/who.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Relative clauses add descriptive detail to nouns. Defining relative clauses (no commas) restrict meaning: 'berries from a particular tree.' Non-defining clauses (with commas) add extra information: 'Coffeehouses, known as qahveh khaneh, became...' The pronoun 'where' introduces place-based relative clauses ('spaces where people gathered'), which is useful for describing settings.

translationVi:
Ít loại đồ uống nào có ảnh hưởng lớn đến lịch sử nhân loại như cà phê. Điều bắt đầu như một khám phá ở vùng cao nguyên Ethiopia hơn một nghìn năm trước đã phát triển thành một trong những mặt hàng được giao dịch rộng rãi nhất thế giới, kết nối nông dân, thương nhân và người uống trên mọi châu lục.

Theo truyền thuyết, một người chăn dê tên Kaldi nhận thấy những con vật của mình trở nên bất thường năng động sau khi ăn quả từ một cây đặc biệt. Ông mang những quả đó đến một tu viện địa phương, nơi các tu sĩ pha thành đồ uống và phát hiện ra nó giúp họ thức suốt giờ cầu kinh buổi tối dài. Từ Ethiopia, cà phê lan sang Bán đảo Ả Rập, nơi nó được trồng trọt và giao dịch rộng rãi. Vào thế kỷ 15, cà phê được trồng ở quận Yemen của Ả Rập và sớm đến Ba Tư, Ai Cập và Thổ Nhĩ Kỳ. Các quán cà phê, được gọi là qahveh khaneh, trở thành không gian xã hội và trí tuệ quan trọng nơi mọi người tụ tập để thảo luận về tin tức, chính trị và ý tưởng. Chúng đôi khi được gọi là 'trường học của người khôn ngoan'.

Khi cà phê đến châu Âu vào thế kỷ 17, nó nhanh chóng thay thế bia và rượu vang trở thành đồ uống buổi sáng được ưa chuộng. Trước cà phê, nhiều người châu Âu bắt đầu ngày của mình bằng rượu, điều không khuyến khích tư duy rõ ràng. Cà phê có tác dụng ngược lại. Khi các quán cà phê lan rộng khắp London, Paris và Vienna, chúng trở thành trung tâm kinh doanh và tranh luận chính trị. Lloyd's of London, thị trường bảo hiểm nổi tiếng, thực ra bắt đầu như một quán cà phê nơi các thương nhân gặp nhau để sắp xếp các thỏa thuận.

Thương mại cà phê xuyên Đại Tây Dương sau đó đã thay đổi nền kinh tế của toàn bộ các khu vực. Các cường quốc châu Âu đã thiết lập đồn điền cà phê ở các thuộc địa của họ ở châu Mỹ, Đông Nam Á và châu Phi. Nhu cầu lao động tại các đồn điền này có liên hệ sâu sắc với lịch sử buôn bán nô lệ, khiến câu chuyện cà phê vừa đáng chú ý vừa đáng lo ngại.

Ngày nay, cà phê là mặt hàng được giao dịch nhiều thứ hai trên thế giới sau dầu mỏ. Hơn hai tỷ tách được uống mỗi ngày. Sự nổi lên của cà phê đặc sản trong những thập kỷ gần đây đã thay đổi cách mọi người nghĩ về nguồn gốc và quy trình sản xuất cà phê. Người tiêu dùng ngày càng đặt câu hỏi về thương mại công bằng, tác động môi trường và phúc lợi của những người nông dân trồng cà phê. Một quả mọng khiêm tốn từ một ngọn đồi Ethiopia đã, qua nhiều thế kỷ, định hình nền kinh tế, châm ngòi cho các cuộc cách mạng tư tưởng và trở thành phần không thể tách rời của cuộc sống hàng ngày của hàng tỷ người.

questions.prompt: Where did coffee originate according to the passage?
questions.explanation: The passage states that coffee's history began 'in the highlands of Ethiopia over a thousand years ago.'
questions.hint: The passage gives a specific geographical location for coffee's origins.

questions.prompt: According to legend, who first discovered the energising effect of coffee berries?
options: A monk at a local monastery
questions.explanation: The legend mentions 'a goat herder named Kaldi' who noticed his goats became energetic after eating the berries.
questions.hint: The legend involves someone watching their animals.

questions.prompt: What did the monks discover when they made a drink from the berries?
options: It helped them stay awake during long evening prayers
It improved their ability to concentrate on studying
questions.explanation: The passage says the monks 'discovered it kept them awake during long evening prayers.'
questions.hint: Think about why monks would want a stimulating drink.

questions.prompt: What were Arabian coffeehouses sometimes called?
questions.explanation: The passage states coffeehouses were 'sometimes called schools of the wise.'
questions.hint: The nickname relates to the intellectual discussions that took place there.

questions.prompt: What did coffee replace as the preferred morning drink in Europe?
questions.explanation: The passage says coffee 'quickly replaced ale and wine as the preferred morning drink.'
questions.hint: Consider what Europeans typically drank before coffee arrived.

questions.prompt: What was Lloyd's of London before it became an insurance market?
options: A bank where traders kept their money
questions.explanation: The passage states 'Lloyd's of London, the famous insurance market, actually began as a coffeehouse where merchants met to arrange deals.'
questions.hint: Lloyd's origins are in an unexpected type of establishment.

questions.prompt: Which troubling historical connection does the passage make with the coffee trade?
options: The link to the slave trade through plantation labour
The monopoly of coffee by powerful merchants
questions.explanation: The passage says 'The demand for labour on these plantations is deeply connected to the history of the slave trade.'
questions.hint: Think about who worked on the colonial coffee plantations.

questions.prompt: How many cups of coffee are drunk worldwide every day?
questions.explanation: The passage states 'More than two billion cups are drunk every day.'
questions.hint: The passage gives a specific number.

questions.prompt: What has the rise of speciality coffee changed?
options: The price of coffee at supermarkets
How people think about the origin and production of their coffee
The types of coffee machines sold in shops
The popularity of tea compared to coffee
questions.explanation: The passage says speciality coffee has 'transformed how people think about where their coffee comes from and how it is produced.'
questions.hint: Speciality coffee has changed consumer awareness, not just taste.

questions.prompt: What questions are consumers increasingly asking about coffee?
options: How to make the perfect espresso at home
Which coffee brands have the best flavour
About fair trade, environmental impact, and farmer welfare
Whether decaf coffee is healthier than regular coffee
questions.explanation: The passage mentions consumers asking 'questions about fair trade, environmental impact, and the welfare of the farmers.'
questions.hint: Modern coffee consumers are concerned with ethics and sustainability.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator describe coffee as being for billions of people today?
options: An inseparable part of daily life
A source of significant health benefits
questions.explanation: The narrator concludes that coffee has become 'an inseparable part of daily life for billions of people.'
questions.hint: The final sentence of the passage answers this question.

cloze.template: Few beverages have had as much influence on human history as coffee. What began as a discovery in the highlands of Ethiopia over a thousand years ago has grown into one of the world's most widely traded {{b1}}, connecting farmers, traders, and drinkers across every continent. According to legend, a goat herder named Kaldi noticed that his animals became unusually {{b2}} after eating berries from a particular tree. From Ethiopia, coffee spread to the Arabian Peninsula, where it was {{b3}} and traded extensively. Coffeehouses became important social and {{b4}} spaces where people gathered to discuss news, politics, and ideas. They were sometimes called 'schools of the wise.' When coffee arrived in Europe in the seventeenth century, it quickly replaced ale and wine as the {{b5}} morning drink. Before coffee, many Europeans began their day with alcohol, which did not exactly encourage {{b6}}. As coffeehouses spread across London, Paris, and Vienna, they became centres of business and political {{b7}}. Lloyd's of London actually began as a coffeehouse where merchants met to {{b8}} deals. European powers established coffee {{b9}} in their colonies. The demand for labour on these plantations is deeply connected to the history of the slave trade, making coffee's story both remarkable and {{b10}}. Today, more than two billion cups are drunk every day. Consumers are increasingly asking questions about fair trade, {{b11}} impact, and the welfare of the farmers who grow their beans.

blanks.explanation: 'Commodities' are raw materials or primary goods that are bought and sold in large quantities — coffee and oil are classic examples.
The legend says the animals 'became unusually energetic' — meaning they had much more energy than normal after eating the coffee berries.
'Cultivated' means grown deliberately through farming — coffee was cultivated (farmed systematically) on the Arabian Peninsula.
'Intellectual spaces' means places where ideas and learning are discussed — the passage describes coffeehouses as centres of knowledge and debate.
'Preferred' means chosen above other options — coffee became the drink that most Europeans chose to have in the morning.
'Clear thinking' means being able to think logically and rationally — alcohol in the morning had the opposite effect.
'Political debate' means the discussion and argument of political ideas — coffeehouses were famous for this kind of intellectual exchange.
'Arrange deals' means to organise and set up business agreements — a natural collocation in commercial contexts.
'Plantations' are large farms established in colonised territories, typically growing one crop like coffee, sugar, or cotton.
The passage describes coffee's story as 'both remarkable and troubling' because its global spread involved the slave trade.
'Environmental impact' refers to the effect of human activity on the natural world — a key concern in modern discussions about coffee production.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The passage describes coffee as both a force for intellectual progress and a commodity linked to the slave trade. How do you think consumers today should respond to learning that some products they enjoy have troubling histories? Does knowing the history of a product change your feelings about consuming it?
